attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring or one-time remote agent ("run this every Monday", "open a cleanup PR for X in 2 weeks"), or to manage existing routines. ALSO OFFER PROACTIVELY: after you finish work that has a natural future follow-up, end your reply with a one-line offer to schedule a background agent to do it. Strong signals: a feature flag / gate / experiment / staged rollout was just shipped (offer a one-time agent in ~2 weeks to open a cleanup PR or evaluate results), a new alert/monitor was created (offer a recurring agent to triage it), a TODO/migration with a "remove once X" condition was left behind (offer a one-time agent to do the removal). Skip the offer for refactors, bug fixes, and anything that is done once it ships. Name a concrete action and cadence ("in 2 weeks", "every Monday") and only offer when the run just succeeded — do not pitch a schedule for something that has not happened yet.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	### Substep protocol — capture for CLAUDE.md (planned for next subcycle)
2	
3	The 0.41.1-6.5 cycle was the first use of the per-substep `@`
4	protocol. It worked well for review/bisection but exposed a
5	contract mismatch with `vc-x1 push`. Capture for CLAUDE.md
6	before the next multi-substep cycle.
7	
8	**Draft text — proposed addition under
9	`## Pre-commit Requirements > ### Versioning`:**
10	
11	> #### Substep protocol (within a multi-step `X.Y.Z-N`)
12	>
13	> For multi-step cycles where a single `X.Y.Z-N` covers several
14	> separable concerns, work each substep on its own `@`:
15	>
16	> ```
17	> jj new -R .   # start substep (1)
18	> ... do substep (1) work + cargo test --bins ...
19	> jj new -R .   # start substep (2)
20	> ... do substep (2) work + cargo test --bins ...
21	> ... etc ...
22	> ```
23	>
24	> At close-out, fold the substep `@`s into one commit and proceed
25	> with the normal `vc-x1 push` flow.
26	>
27	> **Wins observed (0.41.1-6.5):** per-substep diffs stay clean
28	> for review; bisection is a single `jj edit` jump per candidate;
29	> intermediate failure can be its own substep that's visible in
30	> the substep ladder.
31	>
32	> **Hard rule — `cargo test --bins` per substep.** Build + clippy
33	> between substeps isn't enough. A regression introduced in (1)
34	> of -6.5 wasn't caught until (4) ran the full test suite.
35	>
36	> **Close-out — squash + edit.** The bot thinks the cleanest way
37	> to reconcile per-substep `@`s with `vc-x1 push`'s contract
38	> (work-uncommitted-in-`@`) is:
39	>
40	> ```
41	> jj squash --from "@<base>..@" --into @<base> -R .
42	> jj edit @- -R .         # move @ onto the squashed commit
43	> vc-x1 push <bookmark> --yes --title "..." --body "..."
44	> ```
45	>
46	> `@<base>` is `@---` for a 3-substep ladder, `@----` for 4, etc.
47	> The bot thinks `jj op restore <op-before-first-substep>` is an
48	> alternative that collapses all substep work back into `@`
49	> uncommitted, but neither path has been re-validated since the
50	> recovery in -6.5; treat as a working hypothesis until the next
51	> multi-substep cycle exercises it.
52	
53	**Notes for tomorrow's work:**
54	
55	- This lands as its own subcycle (suggest 0.41.1-6.6.0 or whatever
56	  the next `-N` slot is). CLAUDE.md-only edit, no code changes.
57	- Verify the squash + edit recipe above by trying it cleanly on a
58	  scratch `jj new`/`jj new` chain before relying on it for a real
59	  cycle. If `jj op restore` is preferred, validate that path too.
60	- Once verified, drop the "The bot thinks ..." markers and replace
61	  with a confirmed recipe. Note in chores which path was adopted.
62	
63	### Decisions made during design

file.content:
### Substep protocol — capture for CLAUDE.md (planned for next subcycle)

The 0.41.1-6.5 cycle was the first use of the per-substep `@`
protocol. It worked well for review/bisection but exposed a
contract mismatch with `vc-x1 push`. Capture for CLAUDE.md
before the next multi-substep cycle.

**Draft text — proposed addition under
`## Pre-commit Requirements > ### Versioning`:**

> #### Substep protocol (within a multi-step `X.Y.Z-N`)
>
> For multi-step cycles where a single `X.Y.Z-N` covers several
> separable concerns, work each substep on its own `@`:
>
> ```
> jj new -R .   # start substep (1)
> ... do substep (1) work + cargo test --bins ...
> jj new -R .   # start substep (2)
> ... do substep (2) work + cargo test --bins ...
> ... etc ...
> ```
>
> At close-out, fold the substep `@`s into one commit and proceed
> with the normal `vc-x1 push` flow.
>
> **Wins observed (0.41.1-6.5):** per-substep diffs stay clean
> for review; bisection is a single `jj edit` jump per candidate;
> intermediate failure can be its own substep that's visible in
> the substep ladder.
>
> **Hard rule — `cargo test --bins` per substep.** Build + clippy
> between substeps isn't enough. A regression introduced in (1)
> of -6.5 wasn't caught until (4) ran the full test suite.
>
> **Close-out — squash + edit.** The bot thinks the cleanest way
> to reconcile per-substep `@`s with `vc-x1 push`'s contract
> (work-uncommitted-in-`@`) is:
>
> ```
> jj squash --from "@<base>..@" --into @<base> -R .
> jj edit @- -R .         # move @ onto the squashed commit
> vc-x1 push <bookmark> --yes --title "..." --body "..."
> ```
>
> `@<base>` is `@---` for a 3-substep ladder, `@----` for 4, etc.
> The bot thinks `jj op restore <op-before-first-substep>` is an
> alternative that collapses all substep work back into `@`
> uncommitted, but neither path has been re-validated since the
> recovery in -6.5; treat as a working hypothesis until the next
> multi-substep cycle exercises it.

**Notes for tomorrow's work:**

- This lands as its own subcycle (suggest 0.41.1-6.6.0 or whatever
  the next `-N` slot is). CLAUDE.md-only edit, no code changes.
- Verify the squash + edit recipe above by trying it cleanly on a
  scratch `jj new`/`jj new` chain before relying on it for a real
  cycle. If `jj op restore` is preferred, validate that path too.
- Once verified, drop the "The bot thinks ..." markers and replace
  with a confirmed recipe. Note in chores which path was adopted.

### Decisions made during design

# Todo

This file contains near term tasks with a short description
and reference links to more details.

## In Progress

A bulleted list of the in-progress task's development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

- 0.42.0-0 plan + version bump + new chores-07.md (done) [72]
- 0.42.0-1 scope.rs enum — `Roles(Vec<Side>) | Single(PathBuf)` (done) [72]
- 0.42.0-2 custom CLI parser + retrofit `init` --scope (done) [72]
- 0.42.0-3 retrofit `sync` — drop -R, add -s (done) [72]
- 0.42.0-4 push --scope (state-machine becomes scope-aware) (current) [72]
- 0.42.0-5 finalize --scope (replaces --repo) [72]
- 0.42.0-6 clone --scope [72]
- 0.42.0-7 Single(_) dogfood validation [72]
- 0.42.0 close-out [72]

## Todo

A markdown list of tasks to do in the near future, ordered
highest-priority first. Keep entries brief — 1-3 lines.
Detailed motivation, safety requirements, and ordering belong
in `notes/chores-NN.md` design subsections; link via `[N]` ref.

Items use lazy numbering — every entry begins with `1. `; the
markdown renderer auto-numbers them, so reorder/insert without
renumbering. Reference by displayed number ("let's work on #3").
1. Add a vc-x1 validate-repo?
1. vc-x1 push: `--scope=code|bot|code,bot|<path>` flag.
   Lands in the 0.42.0 cycle alongside the enum
   refactor; state machine becomes scope-aware (single-
   side path skips `commit-claude`/bookmark-claude/
   `finalize-claude`; `Single(_)` is single-repo mode).
   [57],[60],[71],[72]
1. vc-x1 clone: `--scope=code|bot|code,bot|<path>` flag.
   Parallel to `init --scope`; single-repo clone target
   via the path form. 0.42.0 cycle. [60],[71],[72]
1. vc-x1 validate-desc / fix-desc:
   `--scope=code|bot|code,bot` flag. Same role vocabulary
   as elsewhere — `code` validates code's commits against
   bot, `bot` reverses, `code,bot` does both (new
   default). `Single(_)` errors here (validate compares
   two repos by definition). 0.42.0 cycle. [60],[71],[72]
1. CommonArgs sweep — add `--scope=code|bot|code,bot|<path>`
   to `chid`/`desc`/`list`/`show` in one cycle (single
   shared `CommonArgs` change picks all four up). Drops
   the existing `-R`/`--repo` repeatable flag in favor of
   the new path form. 0.42.0 cycle. [60],[71],[72]
1. Help layout: force over-under everywhere. Apply
   `next_line_help(true)` at the root (or via the existing
   `cli_with_banner` walker) so every subcommand's `-h` /
   `--help` uses the same layout. Today clap auto-picks
   per-command based on the widest flag spec, so
   `sync -h` is two-column but `init -h` is over-under —
   visual inconsistency.
1. Consider renaming the `.vc-config.toml` `[workspace]`
   section. Rust readers expect `[workspace]` to mean a
   Cargo workspace, which a vc-x1 dual-repo isn't.
   Candidates: `[repo-list]`, `[project]`, `[dual-repo]`.
   Breaking change — needs migration story (read both
   names during a transition cycle, or one-shot rewrite
   in `vc-x1 sync`/`init` on first contact). Drives the
   broader "stop saying workspace in user-facing surfaces"
   sweep.
1. Add `status` (alias `st`) subcommand: `jj st` across both
   repos in one shot. Uses `--scope` from day one. This is
   natural home for the working-copy signal called out and
   it needs to include remotes, like remotes/origin/main. [54].
1. `vc-x1 init --dry-run` should bypass the
   `--repo-remote` path-existence preflight (currently fires
   before the dry-run early-return; observed dogfooding
   2026-04-24).
1. vc-x1 push: `--squash` flag. Squashes WC into `@-` via
   `--ignore-immutable` and force-pushes; needs
   `--force-with-lease`-equivalent + state-sanity preflight in
   place first. [57]
1. vc-x1 push: `--message-file PATH` flag. Git-style commit
   message file (first line = title, blank, rest = body).
   Alternative to `--title` + `--body`. [58]
1. Mirror `--check` / `--no-check` onto `vc-x1 push` (forwards
   through to the preflight `vc-x1 sync` invocation).
   0.37.1 hard-codes `--check`; default stays `--check`.
1. Add `validate-repo` subcommand: diagnostic that runs all
   `verify_*` checks (tracking, push state freshness, ochid
   integrity, conflicts, config sanity, working-copy state)
   and reports per-check pass/fail. Exit code = number of
   failed checks. Implementation: promote
   `verify_state_sanity` / `verify_completion_sanity` from
   push.rs to `common.rs`. [69]
1. sync: surface working-copy state in the up-to-date summary
   (per-repo pending-files count or compact stat). Wording-only
   fix shipped in 0.37.1; this is the design+impl. [54]
1. bm-track silent-when-clean refinement. Print on entry/exit
   only when state isn't fully tracked or when exit state
   differs from entry. [62]
1. "Oh shit" revert — post-success undo via `.vc-x1-ops/`
   anchor dir. Idea-stage; every repo-mutating command drops a
   pre-op snapshot, `vc-x1 undo` restores both repos. [57]
1. Restructure templates: replace separate `vc-template-x1` +
   `vc-template-x1.claude` repos with a single `vc-template-x1`
   that has `.claude/` as a subdir (covers `LICENSE-*` etc. for
   both sides in one place). Updates to `vc-x1 init` / `clone`
   needed for the new layout.
1. Source-code design ref sweep + CLAUDE.md codification:
   adopt section-name + `blob/main/...` URL pattern for source
   code refs to designs; codify in CLAUDE.md alongside the
   existing markdown ref conventions. Sweep targets:
   src/push.rs lines 4, 121, 645, 1219. [68]
1. Richer bookmark enumeration: per-bookmark remote presence + tracking status [52]
1. Per-line/per-thread runtime log points (future, maybe) [36]
1. Add Windows symlink support via `std::os::windows::fs::symlink_dir` [37]
1. Add "::" revision syntax for jj compatibility
1. Add -p, --parents, -c, --children so parent and child counts can be asymmetric
1. Add integration tests in tests/ for subcommands using temp jj repos (tempfile crate)
1. Fix .claude repo history: dev0 through dev2 sessions squashed into wrong commit [4],[5]
1. Add `vc-x1 setup` subcommand: completions install, .claude repo init, symlink setup [27]
1. Add dynamic revision completion via `ArgValueCompleter` (jj doesn't complete revsets either) [28],[29]

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

# Notes

This directory contains various notes and documentation related to the project.
Each file is organized by topic for easy reference.

By default there are chores-*.md and todo.md. Chores are general notes
about tasks and todo.md contains short term tasks and their status.

In the future we I expect we may want to create a "notes"
database to better manage the information, TBD.

Examples chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Create a binary that lists jj info 

This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
and using `jj-lib`
```

## Workflow and conventions

Bot-facing workflow, versioning, and code conventions live in
[`../CLAUDE.md`](../CLAUDE.md). Start there for:

- **Versioning during development** — single-step vs multi-step,
  `-N` pre-release suffixes, done-marker discipline.
- **Code Conventions** — doc comments on every file / fn / method,
  `// OK: …` justifications on `unwrap*` calls, ask-on-ambiguity,
  stuck detection.
- **Commit-Push-Finalize Flow** — two-checkpoint per-step
  discipline with hard stop after finalize.

## Todo format

Todo.md contains two main sections "Todo" and "Done" each item is a
short explanations of a tasks and links to more details using 1 or more
references.

Todo items use lazy numbering — every entry begins with `1. ` and the
markdown renderer auto-numbers them. Reorder or insert items without
renumbering, and reference an entry by its displayed number ("let's
work on #3"). The Done section keeps `-` bullets — items aren't
referenced by number once completed.

Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
In markdown, `[2,3]` is a single ref key (won't resolve) and `[2][3]`
is parsed as display text `2` with ref key `3` (so `[2]` won't resolve).

Examples:

# Todo
- Add new feature X [details](features.md#feature-x)
- Fix bug Y [1]

# Done
- Fixed issue Z [2],[3]

[1]: bugs.md#bug-y
[2]: issues.md#issue-z
[3]: fixes.md#fix-z

## Reference numbering

Every note file (`todo.md`, `chores-NN.md`, `done.md`) keeps a
file-local `# References` section at the bottom. Reference numbers
are scoped to that file — `[1]` in `chores-07.md` and `[1]` in
`chores-01.md` are independent slots that may point at completely
different URLs. New chores files start their numbering at `[1]`.

## Retiring Done entries

`todo.md`'s `## Done` section is a rolling buffer of recently shipped
work, not a permanent log. Move entries into `done.md` at two natural
beats:

- **Closing a ladder** — when the final `X.Y.Z` (no suffix) commit
  ships, decide which prior entries are no longer needed for nearby
  context and migrate them.
- **Opening a new ladder** — at `X.Y.Z-0`, do the same sweep before
  bumping the version.

Migration mechanics:

- Move the bullet itself from `todo.md > ## Done` to
  `done.md` (preserving the original ref number).
- Copy any references the moved entries cite into
  `done.md`'s `# References` section (those refs are file-local,
  so coexisting with `todo.md`'s namespace is fine).
- Prune any references in `todo.md > # References` no longer cited
  by anything in `## In Progress` / `## Todo` / `## Done`. This
  frees the numbers for future reuse.

14	## --scope enum refactor (0.42.0)
15	
16	Picks up the work the 0.41.0 cycle redirected away from. The
17	foundation captured in [2] (chores-06's `### 0.41.0-4: capture
18	--scope enum vocabulary`) lands here as code: the `Scope`
19	type becomes a sum (`Roles(Vec<Side>) | Single(PathBuf)`), the
20	flag surface unifies under `--scope` / `-s` with both keyword and
21	path forms, and every dual-repo-aware command migrates to the
22	new shape in dependency order.
23	
24	**Cycle steps (initial sketch — sub-step boundaries can shift
25	once each command's call sites are seen).**
26	
27	- **0.42.0-0** — this plan + version bump + new
28	  `notes/chores-07.md`. Notes only.
29	- **0.42.0-1** — `scope.rs` enum. Internal only — no
30	  CLI changes yet, no consumers updated. Existing
31	  `Scope(Vec<Side>)` → `enum Scope { Roles(Vec<Side>),
32	  Single(PathBuf) }`. Helpers (`has_code`, `has_bot`,
33	  `is_code_only`, `is_both`, `is_empty`) shift to operate
34	  on `Roles` only; `Single(_)` is a distinct mode. Tests
35	  follow.
36	- **0.42.0-2** — custom CLI value parser for `--scope` +
37	  retrofit `init`. Parser handles the keyword set
38	  (`code|bot|code,bot|bot,code`) and the prefixed-path
39	  form (`./...`, `/...`, `~/...`). Bare names that aren't
40	  keywords error with a "did you mean `./foo`?" hint.
41	  Init is the smallest consumer (it already takes
42	  `--scope=code|bot|code,bot`); migrating it first proves
43	  the parser end-to-end before sync's more complex
44	  resolution logic comes onto the new shape.
45	- **0.42.0-3** — retrofit `sync`. Drop `-R/--repo`. Add
46	  `-s` short form. Default-scope rules update to the
47	  three-state model from [2]: dual workspace →
48	  `Roles([Code, Bot])`, single-repo workspace →
49	  `Roles([Code])`, POR → `Single(cwd)`. `scope_to_repos`
50	  in `common.rs` updates to handle the `Single(_)`
51	  variant. Update sync's integration tests where they
52	  exercise the resolver chain.
53	- **0.42.0-4** — `push --scope`. State machine becomes
54	  scope-aware: each stage either runs or no-ops based on
55	  scope. `Single(<path>)` mode means single-repo push
56	  (no `commit-claude` / `bookmark-claude` /
57	  `finalize-claude`, no `ochid:` trailer). `Roles(...)`
58	  modes work as captured in chores-06 [1]. Persist scope
59	  in `PushState` so resumes use the same scope. Add
60	  integration tests for each scope shape.
61	- **0.42.0-5** — `finalize --scope`. Replace the existing
62	  `--repo` flag with `--scope` (`Roles` for the dual case,
63	  `Single(<path>)` for the single-repo case). `--repo`
64	  goes away; per the 0.41.0-3 capture this is the
65	  intentional break, not a deprecation.
66	- **0.42.0-6** — `clone --scope`. Parallel to init —

69	- **0.42.0-7** — Single(_) dogfood validation. Apply the

71	  single-repo fixture (likely the `vc-template-x1` repo
72	  itself, since it's the canonical single-repo target).
73	  Surface and fix anything the unit tests miss.
74	- **0.42.0 (final)** — cycle close-out. Drops the
75	  `-N` suffix; updates `notes/todo.md`'s In Progress and

foundation captured in [2] (chores-06's `### 0.41.0-4: capture
--scope enum vocabulary`) lands here as code: the `Scope`
type becomes a sum (`Roles(Vec<Side>) | Single(PathBuf)`), the
flag surface unifies under `--scope` / `-s` with both keyword and
path forms, and every dual-repo-aware command migrates to the
new shape in dependency order.

**Cycle steps (initial sketch — sub-step boundaries can shift
once each command's call sites are seen).**

- **0.42.0-0** — this plan + version bump + new
  `notes/chores-07.md`. Notes only.
- **0.42.0-1** — `scope.rs` enum. Internal only — no
  CLI changes yet, no consumers updated. Existing
  `Scope(Vec<Side>)` → `enum Scope { Roles(Vec<Side>),
  Single(PathBuf) }`. Helpers (`has_code`, `has_bot`,
  `is_code_only`, `is_both`, `is_empty`) shift to operate
  on `Roles` only; `Single(_)` is a distinct mode. Tests
  follow.
- **0.42.0-2** — custom CLI value parser for `--scope` +
  retrofit `init`. Parser handles the keyword set
  (`code|bot|code,bot|bot,code`) and the prefixed-path
  form (`./...`, `/...`, `~/...`). Bare names that aren't
  keywords error with a "did you mean `./foo`?" hint.
  Init is the smallest consumer (it already takes
  `--scope=code|bot|code,bot`); migrating it first proves
  the parser end-to-end before sync's more complex
  resolution logic comes onto the new shape.
- **0.42.0-3** — retrofit `sync`. Drop `-R/--repo`. Add
  `-s` short form. Default-scope rules update to the
  three-state model from [2]: dual workspace →
  `Roles([Code, Bot])`, single-repo workspace →
  `Roles([Code])`, POR → `Single(cwd)`. `scope_to_repos`
  in `common.rs` updates to handle the `Single(_)`
  variant. Update sync's integration tests where they
  exercise the resolver chain.

  scope-aware: each stage either runs or no-ops based on
  scope. `Single(<path>)` mode means single-repo push
  (no `commit-claude` / `bookmark-claude` /
  `finalize-claude`, no `ochid:` trailer). `Roles(...)`
  modes work as captured in chores-06 [1]. Persist scope

  integration tests for each scope shape.

  `--repo` flag with `--scope` (`Roles` for the dual case,

1. There is a "problem" or "task" to solve or do and we talk about it and when a direction is decided there is a plan
2. Create a plan and decide if we're going to do a multi-step or single-step solution

So in our last session we added the notion of sub-steps, previously I tended
to start a task by approving every edit and then relaxing that requirement over
the session. But that is tedious for the bot and me and somewhat inconsistent.
I then decided I'd review on a per-file or if it was a refactor when the refactor
was done, but that felt a to course.

So last session we decided to try sub-steps, where step was broken down into
sub-steps and each sub-step but instead of committing a sub-step each sub-step
was performed in a new jj "revision" in memory and then at the end of the
sub-steps we'd squash the sub-steps into a single "revision" do the commit to disk.

This worked pretty well, but the command we used,
`jj squash --from revision-set --into revsion` ended up with a odd and we
had a "hole", the bot feels we close that hole by doing a `jj edit @- -R .`
but we need to validate that's the solution and actually validate if there
is a "hole" maybe we made a mistake in one of the sub-steps.

Soooo the task right now it so create a simple test sequence of multiple sub-steps
and devise an appropriate protocol. Thoughts?

content.text:
Thoughts before we run anything:

**On the "hole"** — I suspect what felt like a hole is just `jj squash`'s default behavior leaving an empty `@` after folding work upward into a parent. The `notes/substep-protocol.md` recipe (`jj squash --from "@<base>..@" --into @<base>`) squashes everything *into* `@<base>` (the original immutable commit before substeps started). Even with `--ignore-immutable` that's the wrong end of the chain — it rewrites the base and leaves the tail empty. Hence the `jj edit @- -R .` patch to move `@` back onto the squashed commit.

**Cleaner direction** — squash *downward* (later commits absorb earlier ones), which naturally lands all the work in `@` exactly where `vc-x1 push` expects it:

```
# State at substep close-out: @base ← s1 ← s2 ← s3=@ (s3 has the last substep's changes)
jj squash --from "@<base>..@-" --into @ -R .
# Result: @base ← @ (with s1+s2+s3 changes), no hole, no extra edit step
```

`@<base>..@-` is the ladder minus the base and minus `@`, all folded into `@`. One command per close-out, regardless of substep count, no rewrite of the immutable base.

**Recommended next move** — write a scratch test under `/tmp` (fresh `jj git init`, dummy file, three substeps each touching distinct lines, run the close-out, verify final state with `jj log` + `jj diff`). Validate both the "downward-squash" recipe and the existing "upward-squash + jj edit" recipe so we can write up the protocol with measured behavior rather than `The bot thinks ...`.

Want me to draft the scratch script as a runnable shell sequence we can step through together, or just spell it out for you to drive?

content.text:
Agree on the shape. A few details to pin down before we build the fixture:

**Scope**: pure `jj+git` scratch repo (no `vc-x1`, no `.claude`). The substep protocol is upstream of `vc-x1 push` — its only contract is "produce a clean `@` ready to commit." If we validate that in plain jj, push consumes it for free. Adding the dual-repo + `.vc-config.toml` + symlink fixture would 3x the setup without testing anything substep-specific.

**Same-file edits**: yes, this is the right stress. Each substep edits the same line(s), so the ladder has real ordering — squash direction matters, and a botched recipe surfaces as content drift, not just topology drift. Suggest a 10-line `counter.txt` where each substep changes a different line, plus one substep that *re-edits* a line a prior substep touched (forces a real conflict-free merge of overlapping edits).

**"Local remote"**: `git init --bare /tmp/substep-test-remote.git`, add as `origin` in the scratch repo, push via `jj git push`. After push, clone it elsewhere and `git log` to confirm the published history is what we intended (single squashed commit on top of `@base`, no orphan substep commits, no rewrite of `@base`).

**What we test**: both candidate recipes side-by-side, on identical fixtures, so we can compare:
1. *Downward squash* — `jj squash --from "@base..@-" --into @` (one command)
2. *Upward squash + edit* — `jj squash --from "@base..@" --into @base` (the existing draft) followed by `jj edit @-`

For each: dump `jj log -r ::@`, `jj diff -r @`, then `jj git push` and inspect the bare remote.

**Out of scope**: `vc-x1 push` itself, dual-repo behavior, ochid trailers. Those plug in once the substep close-out shape is settled.

Sound right? If yes I'll write the fixture as a single shell script we can run and inspect, with a `--reset` flag to nuke and rebuild the scratch dirs between recipe runs.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Substep close-out fixture.
#
# Runs a 4-substep ladder editing the same file in a scratch jj+git repo,
# applies one of the candidate close-out recipes, and pushes to a local
# bare remote so we can verify the published history.
#
# Substeps:
#   1: line-1 zero  -> one
#   2: line-2 zero  -> two
#   3: line-3 zero  -> three
#   4: line-1 one   -> four    (re-edits substep 1's line; tests diff composition)
#
# Recipes:
#   downward  jj squash --from "BASE..@-" --into @            (one command)
#   upward    jj squash --from "BASE..@"  --into BASE
#             jj edit @-                                       (existing draft in
#                                                              substep-protocol.md)
#
# Layout under /tmp/substep-test/:
#   work/          working repo (jj+git colocated)
#   remote.git/    bare git remote (acts as origin)
#   verify/        fresh clone of remote.git used to inspect published history

set -euo pipefail

ROOT=/tmp/substep-test
WORK=$ROOT/work
REMOTE=$ROOT/remote.git
VERIFY=$ROOT/verify

usage() {
    cat <<EOF
usage: $0 [--reset] {downward|upward}

  downward  jj squash --from "BASE..@-" --into @
  upward    jj squash --from "BASE..@"  --into BASE --ignore-immutable
            jj edit @-

  --reset   nuke $ROOT before running (so each recipe starts from a fresh fixture)
EOF
    exit 1
}

RESET=0
RECIPE=""
while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
    case "$1" in
        --reset) RESET=1; shift ;;
        downward|upward) RECIPE=$1; shift ;;
        -h|--help) usage ;;
        *) echo "unknown arg: $1" >&2; usage ;;
    esac
done

[[ -z "$RECIPE" ]] && usage

if [[ $RESET -eq 1 ]]; then
    rm -rf "$ROOT"
fi

mkdir -p "$ROOT"

# --- bare remote ---
if [[ ! -d "$REMOTE" ]]; then
    echo "::: creating bare remote at $REMOTE"
    git init --bare "$REMOTE" >/dev/null
fi

# --- working repo ---
if [[ ! -d "$WORK" ]]; then
    echo "::: creating working repo at $WORK"
    mkdir -p "$WORK"
    cd "$WORK"
    jj git init --colocate >/dev/null

    cat > counter.txt <<'EOF'
line-1: zero
line-2: zero
line-3: zero
line-4: zero
line-5: zero
line-6: zero
line-7: zero
line-8: zero
line-9: zero
line-10: zero
EOF
    jj describe -m "initial: counter file" >/dev/null
    jj new >/dev/null

    git remote add origin "$REMOTE"
    jj bookmark create main -r @-
    jj git push --bookmark main
fi

cd "$WORK"

BASE_CHID=$(jj log -r @- --no-graph -T 'change_id ++ "\n"' | head -1)
echo "::: BASE_CHID=$BASE_CHID"

dump_state() {
    local label="$1"
    echo
    echo "===== $label ====="
    jj log -r "::@"
    echo "----- counter.txt (working copy) -----"
    cat counter.txt
}

# --- 4 substeps ---

sed -i 's/line-1: zero/line-1: one/' counter.txt
jj describe -m "substep-1: line-1 -> one" >/dev/null
dump_state "after substep 1"

jj new >/dev/null
sed -i 's/line-2: zero/line-2: two/' counter.txt
jj describe -m "substep-2: line-2 -> two" >/dev/null
dump_state "after substep 2"

jj new >/dev/null
sed -i 's/line-3: zero/line-3: three/' counter.txt
jj describe -m "substep-3: line-3 -> three" >/dev/null
dump_state "after substep 3"

jj new >/dev/null
sed -i 's/line-1: one/line-1: four/' counter.txt
jj describe -m "substep-4: re-edit line-1 -> four" >/dev/null
dump_state "after substep 4 (pre close-out)"

# --- close-out ---
echo
echo "===== close-out: $RECIPE ====="
case "$RECIPE" in
    downward)
        echo "+ jj squash --from \"$BASE_CHID..@-\" --into @"
        jj squash --from "$BASE_CHID..@-" --into @
        ;;
    upward)
        echo "+ jj squash --from \"$BASE_CHID..@\" --into \"$BASE_CHID\" --ignore-immutable"
        jj squash --from "$BASE_CHID..@" --into "$BASE_CHID" --ignore-immutable
        echo "+ jj edit @-"
        jj edit @-
        ;;
esac
dump_state "after close-out"

# --- finalize and push ---
echo
echo "===== finalize: jj commit + bookmark + push ====="
jj commit -m "feat: counter substeps squashed"
jj bookmark set main -r @-
jj git push --bookmark main || {
    echo "::: push failed; retrying"
    jj git push --bookmark main
}

# --- verify by cloning fresh ---
rm -rf "$VERIFY"
git clone -q "$REMOTE" "$VERIFY"
echo
echo "===== published history (fresh clone of $REMOTE) ====="
( cd "$VERIFY" && git log --oneline )
echo
echo "----- counter.txt at remote HEAD -----"
cat "$VERIFY/counter.txt"

```

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Build a 4-revision substep ladder for manual squash inspection.
#
# State after this script:
#   bookmark "base" -> empty commit (no counter.txt)
#   r1   counter.txt = "line 1 count 0"
#   r2   counter.txt = "line 1 count 1"
#   r3   counter.txt = "line 1 count 2"
#   r4   counter.txt = "line 1 count 3"   (@ sits here)
#
# Then run squash recipes by hand from /tmp/substep-test, e.g.:
#
#   jj squash --from "base..@-" --into @                            # downward
#
#   jj squash --from "base..@" --into base --ignore-immutable       # upward
#   jj edit @-

set -euo pipefail

ROOT=/tmp/substep-test
rm -rf "$ROOT"
mkdir -p "$ROOT"
cd "$ROOT"

jj git init --colocate >/dev/null

jj describe -m "base (empty)" >/dev/null
jj bookmark create base -r @
jj new >/dev/null

echo "line 1 count 0" > counter.txt
jj describe -m "r1: count 0" >/dev/null

jj new >/dev/null
echo "line 1 count 1" > counter.txt
jj describe -m "r2: count 1" >/dev/null

jj new >/dev/null
echo "line 1 count 2" > counter.txt
jj describe -m "r3: count 2" >/dev/null

jj new >/dev/null
echo "line 1 count 3" > counter.txt
jj describe -m "r4: count 3" >/dev/null

echo
echo "::: cwd=$ROOT"
echo
jj log -r "::@"
echo "----- counter.txt -----"
cat counter.txt

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Substep close-out fixture.
#
# Runs a 4-substep ladder editing the same file in a scratch jj+git repo,
# applies one of the candidate close-out recipes, and pushes to a local
# bare remote so we can verify the published history.
#
# Substeps:
#   1: line-1 zero  -> one
#   2: line-2 zero  -> two
#   3: line-3 zero  -> three
#   4: line-1 one   -> four    (re-edits substep 1's line; tests diff composition)
#
# Recipes:
#   downward  jj squash --from "BASE..@-" --into @            (one command)
#   upward    jj squash --from "BASE..@"  --into BASE
#             jj edit @-                                       (existing draft in
#                                                              substep-protocol.md)
#
# Layout under /tmp/substep-test/:
#   work/          working repo (jj+git colocated)
#   remote.git/    bare git remote (acts as origin)
#   verify/        fresh clone of remote.git used to inspect published history

set -euo pipefail

ROOT=/tmp/substep-test
WORK=$ROOT/work
REMOTE=$ROOT/remote.git
VERIFY=$ROOT/verify

usage() {
    cat <<EOF
usage: $0 [--reset] {downward|upward}

  downward  jj squash --from "BASE..@-" --into @
  upward    jj squash --from "BASE..@"  --into BASE --ignore-immutable
            jj edit @-

  --reset   nuke $ROOT before running (so each recipe starts from a fresh fixture)
EOF
    exit 1
}

RESET=0
RECIPE=""
while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
    case "$1" in
        --reset) RESET=1; shift ;;
        downward|upward) RECIPE=$1; shift ;;
        -h|--help) usage ;;
        *) echo "unknown arg: $1" >&2; usage ;;
    esac
done

[[ -z "$RECIPE" ]] && usage

if [[ $RESET -eq 1 ]]; then
    rm -rf "$ROOT"
fi

mkdir -p "$ROOT"

# --- bare remote ---
if [[ ! -d "$REMOTE" ]]; then
    echo "::: creating bare remote at $REMOTE"
    git init --bare "$REMOTE" >/dev/null
fi

# --- working repo ---
if [[ ! -d "$WORK" ]]; then
    echo "::: creating working repo at $WORK"
    mkdir -p "$WORK"
    cd "$WORK"
    jj git init --colocate >/dev/null

    cat > counter.txt <<'EOF'
line-1: zero
line-2: zero
line-3: zero
line-4: zero
line-5: zero
line-6: zero
line-7: zero
line-8: zero
line-9: zero
line-10: zero
EOF
    jj describe -m "initial: counter file" >/dev/null
    jj new >/dev/null

    git remote add origin "$REMOTE"
    jj bookmark create main -r @-
    jj git push --bookmark main
fi

cd "$WORK"

BASE_CHID=$(jj log -r @- --no-graph -T 'change_id ++ "\n"' | head -1)
echo "::: BASE_CHID=$BASE_CHID"

dump_state() {
    local label="$1"
    echo
    echo "===== $label ====="
    jj log -r "::@"
    echo "----- counter.txt (working copy) -----"
    cat counter.txt
}

# --- 4 substeps ---

sed -i 's/line-1: zero/line-1: one/' counter.txt
jj describe -m "substep-1: line-1 -> one" >/dev/null
dump_state "after substep 1"

jj new >/dev/null
sed -i 's/line-2: zero/line-2: two/' counter.txt
jj describe -m "substep-2: line-2 -> two" >/dev/null
dump_state "after substep 2"

jj new >/dev/null
sed -i 's/line-3: zero/line-3: three/' counter.txt
jj describe -m "substep-3: line-3 -> three" >/dev/null
dump_state "after substep 3"

jj new >/dev/null
sed -i 's/line-1: one/line-1: four/' counter.txt
jj describe -m "substep-4: re-edit line-1 -> four" >/dev/null
dump_state "after substep 4 (pre close-out)"

# --- close-out ---
echo
echo "===== close-out: $RECIPE ====="
case "$RECIPE" in
    downward)
        echo "+ jj squash --from \"$BASE_CHID..@-\" --into @"
        jj squash --from "$BASE_CHID..@-" --into @
        ;;
    upward)
        echo "+ jj squash --from \"$BASE_CHID..@\" --into \"$BASE_CHID\" --ignore-immutable"
        jj squash --from "$BASE_CHID..@" --into "$BASE_CHID" --ignore-immutable
        echo "+ jj edit @-"
        jj edit @-
        ;;
esac
dump_state "after close-out"

# --- finalize and push ---
echo
echo "===== finalize: jj commit + bookmark + push ====="
jj commit -m "feat: counter substeps squashed"
jj bookmark set main -r @-
jj git push --bookmark main || {
    echo "::: push failed; retrying"
    jj git push --bookmark main
}

# --- verify by cloning fresh ---
rm -rf "$VERIFY"
git clone -q "$REMOTE" "$VERIFY"
echo
echo "===== published history (fresh clone of $REMOTE) ====="
( cd "$VERIFY" && git log --oneline )
echo
echo "----- counter.txt at remote HEAD -----"
cat "$VERIFY/counter.txt"

Yes, that's a valid distinction. Two scenarios that both arise:

**Scenario X — substeps continue work above a prior commit** (typical multi-step cycle):
- Prior pushed commit `P` is immutable.
- Substeps r1..r4 are new work above `P`.
- Close-out: fold r1..r4 into ONE commit on top of `P`. Target = either r1 (oldest substep keeps chid) or r4=@ (newest keeps chid). `P` untouched.

**Scenario Y — substeps ARE the initial work** (your reading of the current fixture):
- No prior commit; r1..r4 are the only substeps and the result should be one commit with no parent above root.
- Close-out: fold everything into one commit. Since target's chid survives, target = r1=base gives your sketch.
- "Rewriting base" isn't a problem because `base` was never pushed — it's local-only, mutable.

Your fixture (`base` bookmark on r1, no separate prior commit) literally encodes Scenario Y, so your sketch is the right answer for it. My earlier prediction was answering Scenario X, which the fixture doesn't represent. My bad.

For Scenario Y, my prediction for `jj squash --from "base..@" --into base`:

```
@  klukzvou wink@saville.com <today> <NEW_COMMIT_HASH> base
│  r1: count 0           ← description likely stays r1's; uncertain (may concat / open editor)
◆  zzzzzzzz root() 00000000
```

Reasoning:
- `base..@` = `{r2, r3, r4}` (r4 = @ included; `..` is inclusive on the right).
- Diffs from r2/r3/r4 fold into base. base ends up with `counter.txt = line 1 count 3`.
- r2, r3, r4 become empty → abandoned.
- `@` lands on `base` (the commit @ pointed to was r4, which is gone; jj rewires @ to the survivor that absorbed it).
- `base` bookmark: stays at klukzvou (chid).
- Commit hash on `base` changes (content changed).
- `--ignore-immutable` not needed because base hasn't been pushed.

**Same two uncertainties as before** about the description and abandoned-commit visibility — squash docs say it concatenates source descriptions and opens `$EDITOR`, so we may get prompted unless we pass `-m`.

Nice catch on the framing. Two observations to add:

**On `--into @` default ambiguity**: the docs page says default is `@`, but bare `jj squash` (no flags) clearly squashes `@` into `@-`. So either the docs are slightly imprecise or jj has a special-case "if both defaulted, treat as `-r @`" rule. Either way, the *observed* default behavior is `--from @ --into @-`.

**Your "moving @" framing actually works** and is illuminating. If you read `@` as a moving reference (pre-state vs post-state):

- Pre-state: `--from @ --into @-` — squash source-@ into its parent.
- Post-state: the old `@-` is now under a fresh empty `@`, so the destination *now occupies the old @-'s slot, which is the parent of the new @*. From the post-state's frame, "into @-" still describes where the work landed.

The "moving @" interpretation also makes the internal model consistent: jj's working-copy reference always points to "the editable WC commit," and after a squash the WC slot gets re-manufactured above the survivor.

**The implication that matters here**: bare `jj squash` is **structurally Y-shape**:
- Source consumed, destination preserved + enriched.
- Fresh empty `@` above destination.
- "Hole" is the natural shape of the operation — not a bug, just an artifact.

This is why the substep close-out wants X explicitly: X *inverts* the natural direction (folds a parent-side ancestor *into* @, keeping the descendant as survivor) so the work stays in `@` and the prior pushed commit (`base`) is left alone. X is the deliberately-against-the-grain recipe; Y is what falls out of jj's defaults.

content.text:
Ah — your "squash myw into utz" phrasing is the *opposite* of jj's `--from X --into Y` semantics.

In jj: `--into Y` means Y is the destination = survivor; the source's diff moves *into* Y. So "squash X into Y" → X disappears, Y stays.

Your desired result keeps `myw` and abandons `utz`. So in jj-flag terms, that's the inverse: `--from utz --into myw`. Reading the flags out loud: "squash utz into myw" — utz disappears, myw stays.

The command:

```
jj squash --from @-- --into @- -u
```

(`@--` = utz, `@-` = myw; `-u` keeps myw's description.)

Predicted result (matches your sketch):

```
@  vptmrpoq ... f374d183 (or new hash; parent unchanged so might stay)
│  r4: count 3
○  mywxpnxs ... <NEW_HASH>             (chid preserved; content rewires baseline; hash changes)
│  r3: count 2                         (description preserved via -u; effective vs base is now "0→2")
○  nkxpuszt ... base 3deef59c
│  r1: count 0                         (untouched)
◆  zzzzzzzz root()
```

- utz's diff (count 0 → 1) folds into myw. myw now has "count 0 → 2" net delta from base.
- myw's parent rewires from utz to base.
- utz becomes empty → abandoned.
- myw chid preserved (this is what you wanted); commit hash changes due to the parent rewire.
- `@`=r4 unaffected: still parent of myw, chid stays, content stays "count 3".

So the takeaway on naming: **jj's `--into` always names the survivor**. The English "X into Y" maps to "X is consumed, Y survives" if you read it as flag-shorthand, which is the opposite of "X is rolled up *with* Y where X stays" if you read it as informal English. Easy to get reversed.

Two issues with that command:

**1. `myw..utz` evaluates to empty.** jj's `X..Y` is `::Y & ~::X` — "ancestors of Y that are not ancestors of X." Since utz is an *ancestor* of myw (utz is older, myw is younger), every ancestor of utz is also an ancestor of myw. So:
- Ancestors of utz: `{utz, base, root}`
